meta.name: Foreign Body Airway Obstruction — Failed BLS

history.hpi: 58-year-old male at a backyard BBQ who was eating a rare steak when he suddenly grabbed his throat and made the universal choking sign. Bystanders immediately initiated abdominal thrusts but were unsuccessful after multiple attempts. Patient became unconscious and was placed supine on the grass. Bystander CPR started.
history.events: Choking event during eating. Bystander abdominal thrusts unsuccessful. Now unconscious.

scene.dispatch: Medic 11, respond code 3 to 842 Maple Lane, for a 58-year-old male choking, now unconscious, CPR in progress by bystanders.
scene.location: Backyard of a private residence. Patient supine on the grass next to a picnic table. BBQ still going nearby.
scene.safety: Scene safe. BBQ flame nearby; move patient if safer working space exists.
scene.bystanders: Multiple bystanders, one is performing chest compressions. The host witnessed the choking event.
visualCues: Adult male supine on the lawn, cyanotic
Visible food particles around the mouth
No air movement observed during compressions
Partial upper denture visible in the oral cavity
Steak and paper plate next to the patient

phases.name: Arrival — Complete Airway Obstruction, Unconscious
phases.description: Unconscious FBAO with no air movement. Protocol algorithm shifts at unconsciousness: chest compressions (which generate airway pressure similar to Heimlich), then look-in-mouth between compressions, direct laryngoscopy with Magill forceps to retrieve the object under visualization. If retrieval fails and the object cannot be removed or advanced, cricothyrotomy is the final answer.

clinicalPresentation.airway: Complete obstruction — no air movement with BVM attempts; obstruction presumed below cords
clinicalPresentation.circulation: Bradycardic pulse present but weakening; CPR in progress
otherFindings: Meat bolus suspected based on history
No air entry with BVM — zero chest rise
EtCO2 reads zero confirming no ventilation

expectedActions.action: Attempt BVM with head tilt/jaw thrust — confirm NO chest rise and no EtCO2 waveform consistent with complete obstruction
Remove the partial upper denture before any airway instrumentation
Continue chest compressions — generate intrathoracic pressure that may dislodge the object (functional equivalent of Heimlich in unconscious patient)
Perform direct laryngoscopy between compression cycles and use Magill forceps to grasp and remove the visualized food bolus
expectedActions.rationale: With the airway open, DL with Magill forceps is the standard for visualized foreign bodies above or at the cords.

expectedActions.action: Continuous suction available with large-bore Yankauer
Apply cardiac monitor, pads (anticipate cardiac arrest)

phases.name: Laryngoscopy Decision Point
phases.description: Direct laryngoscopy is performed. The obstruction is either visualized above the cords (retrievable with Magill) or cannot be visualized because it has passed through the cords and is now subglottic. This is the critical decision point — the crew must commit to retrieval, to right mainstem advancement with an ETT, or to surgical airway.

transitions.condition: Object visualized and removed with Magill forceps
Object is subglottic and cannot be retrieved
Object cannot be advanced or retrieved

clinicalPresentation.airway: Laryngoscopy in progress; visibility improving with suction
otherFindings: Meat bolus visualized at or just below the cords

expectedActions.action: If object is visible above or at the cords, grasp it with Magill forceps and remove under direct vision

If object cannot be retrieved and is subglottic, advance an endotracheal tube into the right mainstem bronchus to push the object distally, then withdraw tube to mid-trachea for single-lung ventilation of the left lung
expectedActions.rationale: When retrieval fails, force the object into one mainstem bronchus and ventilate the other lung — a recognized rescue technique.

expectedActions.action: If the obstruction is at the cords and cannot be advanced or retrieved, proceed with emergent cricothyrotomy

phases.name: Default Path — Successful Magill Retrieval
phases.description: Large meat bolus retrieved from above the cords with Magill forceps. Immediate air movement returns with BVM. Patient regains pulse and pinks up rapidly. Manage as post-arrest with careful airway observation for aspiration and glottic edema.

clinicalPresentation.breathing: BVM-assisted with easy compliance, symmetric chest rise
clinicalPresentation.circulation: Pulse returning with chest compressions halted; ROSC
otherFindings: Patient may regain consciousness — prepare for confusion

expectedActions.action: Keep OPA in place and support ventilation; prepare to intubate if patient does not protect airway
Manage as post-arrest if sustained ROSC — watch for re-arrest and hypotension
Transport to ED with early notification for ENT evaluation of glottic trauma and aspiration

phases.name: Branch: Single-Lung Ventilation After Forced Advancement
phases.description: Object cannot be retrieved. Crew advances ETT deliberately into the right mainstem bronchus to push the obstruction distally, then withdraws tube to mid-trachea. Patient is ventilated through the left lung only while the obstructed right lung is bypassed until hospital bronchoscopy.
phases.triggerCondition: Object cannot be retrieved; advancement chosen

clinicalPresentation.airway: ETT now at 20 cm at the teeth (withdrawn after mainstem advance)
clinicalPresentation.breathing: Single-lung ventilation — breath sounds on the left only
otherFindings: Absent breath sounds on the right (obstructed)
Clear breath sounds on the left
EtCO2 waveform returns, lower than expected
Oxygenation suboptimal due to V/Q mismatch

expectedActions.action: Confirm single-lung ventilation with capnography and unilateral breath sounds; document approach
Emergent transport to a facility with ED bronchoscopy or OR access; early notification
Ventilate gently to avoid barotrauma on the single lung

phases.name: Branch: Surgical Cricothyrotomy
phases.description: Object is at the cords, cannot be retrieved, cannot be advanced, and ventilation is impossible from above. Crich is the only remaining option.
phases.triggerCondition: Can't remove, can't advance, can't ventilate

clinicalPresentation.airway: Surgical cricothyrotomy in place with 6.0 mm cuffed tube
otherFindings: Minor bleeding at surgical site, controlled with gauze pressure

expectedActions.action: Confirm cricothyrotomy placement with waveform capnography and bilateral breath sounds
Transport emergently to a facility with ENT or general surgery for definitive airway management

criticalActions: Recognize complete airway obstruction in an unconscious patient
Perform direct laryngoscopy with Magill forceps to attempt retrieval
Continue chest compressions throughout (they generate airway pressure)
If Magill retrieval fails: choose between advancing ETT to right mainstem OR surgical cricothyrotomy based on visualization
If obstruction is at the cords and cannot be moved: commit to cricothyrotomy without delay
expectedActions: Establish IV access once airway is secured
Manage post-arrest care if ROSC is achieved
bonusActions: Articulate the rationale for single-lung ventilation via forced advancement
Use SALAD technique if blood or vomitus present
Document time to decisive action at each phase

learningObjectives: Use direct laryngoscopy and Magill forceps to retrieve a visualized obstruction
Understand the rationale for forced distal advancement when retrieval fails
Identify when to commit to surgical airway
Avoid algorithm paralysis in a time-critical airway
discussionQuestions: Why is pushing the object distal with an ETT a reasonable rescue maneuver?
How do you decide between advancement and cricothyrotomy?
What are the consequences of single-lung ventilation during transport?
What role do chest compressions play in an unconscious FBAO?
How would your approach differ if this patient had known laryngeal cancer?
commonPitfalls: Continuing abdominal thrusts in an unconscious patient instead of starting compressions
Failing to remove dentures before laryngoscopy
Multiple unsuccessful Magill attempts instead of committing to an alternate strategy
Hesitating to perform cricothyrotomy in a can't-ventilate scenario
Forgetting that chest compressions substitute for abdominal thrusts in the unconscious patient
keyTakeaways: Magill forceps under DL is the first-line tool for visualized FBAO
When retrieval fails, forced advancement or cricothyrotomy are both acceptable
Every second counts — commit, don't deliberate
Compressions + DL + Magill is the unconscious FBAO rhythm
